note: General information for small Kenyan businesses (MSMEs). This data is for educational purposes only and reflects the law and thresholds as of early 2026. Always consult a certified accountant or legal professional for advice specific to your situation.

sections.title: KRA PIN Registration
sections.summary: Every business and individual earning income in Kenya must have a KRA Personal Identification Number (PIN). Registration is FREE and done online via the iTax portal at itax.kra.go.ke. You need: a valid National ID, mobile number, and email address. For a business (sole trader), your personal PIN is used. For a limited company, a separate company PIN is registered. A KRA PIN is required to open a business bank account, apply for tenders, import/export goods, and file tax returns.

sections.title: iTax Portal (Filing Returns)
sections.summary: iTax (itax.kra.go.ke) is the Kenya Revenue Authority's online self-service portal. All taxpayers — including small businesses — must file annual income tax returns by 30 June each year. Even if you had no income, you must file a 'NIL return' to avoid a penalty of KES 2,000. iTax also handles: VAT returns (monthly), PAYE returns (monthly), withholding tax, and instalment tax. First-time users must register their PIN on iTax with a new password.

sections.title: VAT (Value Added Tax)
sections.summary: VAT is charged at 16% on taxable goods and services. Small businesses must register for VAT once their annual turnover exceeds KES 5,000,000 (5 million shillings). Below this threshold, VAT registration is voluntary. Basic food items (unprocessed food like fresh vegetables, fruits, maize flour, bread) are VAT EXEMPT — meaning a mama mboga selling fresh produce does NOT charge VAT on those items. Once registered, businesses must file VAT returns by the 20th of the following month.

sections.title: Income Tax (Individual / Sole Trader)
sections.summary: Sole traders (including MSMEs not registered as companies) pay income tax based on net profit, using the Pay-As-You-Earn (PAYE) equivalent bands: KES 0–288,000/year = 10%; KES 288,001–388,000 = 25%; KES 388,001–6,000,000 = 30%; KES 6,000,001–9,600,000 = 32.5%; Above KES 9,600,000 = 35%. A monthly personal relief of KES 2,400 applies to all individuals. MSMEs with turnover between KES 1,000,000 and KES 50,000,000 may opt for the Turnover Tax (TOT) at 3% of gross revenue instead of the standard income tax — this is simpler for small traders.

sections.title: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) for Employers
sections.summary: If your business employs staff, you must deduct and remit PAYE on their salaries to KRA monthly. PAYE is due by the 9th of the following month via iTax. You must also deduct and remit NSSF (National Social Security Fund) contributions — KES 200/employee/month for Tier 1 — and SHIF (Social Health Insurance Fund, formerly NHIF) contributions based on salary. Failure to deduct or remit these attracts penalties of 25% of the unpaid amount plus 1% interest per month.

sections.title: Business Registration (eCitizen)
sections.summary: In Kenya, you can register a business through the Business Registration Service (BRS) on eCitizen (ecitizen.go.ke). Options include: (1) Sole Proprietorship / Business Name – simplest, costs KES 950, done online in 1–3 days; (2) Partnership – for 2–20 partners; (3) Private Limited Company – provides limited liability, costs ~KES 10,650, takes 3–7 days. Most small market traders operate as sole proprietors. A registered business name protects your brand, is needed for M-Pesa Paybill/Till registration, and is required for formal contracts and tenders.

sections.title: Hustler Fund (Financial Inclusion Fund)
sections.summary: The Kenya Financial Inclusion Fund (Hustler Fund) provides mobile-based credit to Kenyans via mobile money. Access via M-Pesa menu: *254#. Available tiers: Personal loan (KES 500–50,000), Micro business loan (KES 50,000–500,000), SME loan (KES 500,000–2,000,000). Interest rate: 8% per annum (simple interest). Repayment period: Personal = 14 days; Business = 30–90 days. 5% of each loan drawdown is saved into a long-term savings account. To qualify: active M-Pesa account, Kenyan citizen (verified via Huduma Number/ID). Your credit score on the Hustler Fund improves with timely repayments and grows your borrowing limit.

sections.title: SACCOs (Savings and Credit Co-operatives)
sections.summary: SACCOs are member-owned financial co-operatives regulated by the SACCO Societies Regulatory Authority (SASRA). They offer: savings accounts with dividends, loans at 1%/month (12% per annum) — much cheaper than mobile loans, share capital accumulation, and insurance products. Popular SACCOs for traders: Stima SACCO, Unaitas, Kenya Police SACCO, and county-based SACCOs. To join: pay a one-time membership fee (varies), commit to regular monthly savings (as low as KES 500/month). After 3–6 months of saving, you can borrow up to 3× your savings at favourable rates. SACCOs are among the best financial tools for Kenyan SME owners.

sections.title: County Business Permit
sections.summary: All businesses operating within a county must obtain a Single Business Permit (SBP) from their County Government each year. The fee varies by county, business type, and size — for a small market stall in Nairobi it typically starts at KES 5,000–15,000 per year. The SBP is renewed annually and covers trading, health, and fire safety compliance. Apply at your county government offices or, increasingly, via the county's online portal. Operating without a permit can result in fines or stall closure.
